title: Magnomechanical Coupling in Suspended 2D van der Waals Ferromagnets
authors: Ritesh Das, Alvaro Bermejillo-Seco, Herre S. J. van der Zant, Peter G. Steeneken, Yaroslav M. Blanter
abstract: Magnomechanical systems provide a promising route for exploring coherent hybrid magnon-phonon interactions and hybrid information processing, but their realization has so far been limited by weak magnon-phonon coupling in conventional bulk platforms. We show that a suspended membrane of a two-dimensional van der Waals ferromagnet with in-plane magnetization and out-of-plane mechanical oscillations exhibits large magnomechanical coupling dominated by magnetoelastic interactions. The parametric single magnon-phonon coupling rate scales linearly with pre-strain and can reach hundreds of Hertz to low kiloHertz in suspended membranes of van der Waals ferromagnets such as CrGeTe_3 under experimentally realistic conditions. This rate exceeds typical values reported for YIG spheres by more than three orders of magnitude. Our results demonstrate that suspended membranes of van der Waals magnets provide a robust and highly tunable platform for magnomechanics.
summary: This paper proposes using suspended 2D van der Waals ferromagnets as a new platform for magnomechanics. By utilizing the large magnetoelastic coupling and the ability to induce pre-strain via electrostatic gating, the authors demonstrate coupling rates significantly higher than those found in traditional YIG-based systems. The study shows that the coupling strength can be tuned by orders of magnitude through voltage-induced deformation. This makes these membranes a robust candidate for developing chip-scale hybrid quantum information processing devices.
main_problem: The weak magnon-phonon coupling in conventional bulk platforms like YIG spheres limits the realization of efficient, coherent magnomechanical systems for hybrid information processing.
main_result: Suspended 2D van der Waals ferromagnetic membranes, such as CrGeTe3, exhibit magnomechanical coupling rates up to three orders of magnitude higher than YIG spheres and are highly tunable via electrostatic gating.
method: The authors use a theoretical framework involving the Holstein-Primakoff transformation and linearized magnetoelastic interactions, supplemented by COMSOL Multiphysics simulations to model electrostatic-induced deformation.
model_or_system: A suspended, doubly-clamped 2D van der Waals ferromagnetic membrane (specifically CrGeTe3) with in-plane magnetization, subject to out-of-plane mechanical oscillations and electrostatic pre-strain.
key_observables: Single magnon-phonon coupling rate (g0), mechanical eigenfrequency, cooperativity (C), and static deformation (h).
important_parameters: Magnon frequencies in the GHz scale, mechanical frequencies in the 10-100 MHz range, coupling rates in the Hz to kHz range, and pre-strain scaling linearly with coupling.
main_assumptions: The model assumes the thin membrane limit (Lz << Ly < Lx), focuses on the Kittel mode, neglects higher-order strain components, and assumes small displacement amplitudes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the 3D and 2D geometry of the membrane; Figure 2 illustrates ferromagnetic resonance and spin alignment; Figure 4 shows coupling vs. pre-strain; Figure 5 compares COMSOL simulations to analytical models for frequency and coupling strength.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of weak coupling in bulk systems, proposes the 2D vdW membrane platform, derives the analytical coupling mechanism and scaling laws, validates the model with numerical simulations of electrostatic tuning, and concludes with the potential for hybrid quantum technologies.
why_interesting: The platform offers a highly tunable, high-cooperativity interface between magnons and phonons, which is a key building block for hybrid quantum transducers and quantum memory in quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: The magnomechanical system. (a) 3D view of the suspended ferromagnet. The magnons are aligned in-plane via an external magnetic field along y. (b) 2D cross-section of the magnomechanical system. The magnet is pre-strained by pulling it down vertically by a distance h and oscillates around this position with amplitude ∆z. The mechanical displacements are exag- gerated for clarity.

FIG. 2: Ferromagnetic resonance frequency of CrGeTe3 vs. an applied in-plane external field. The insets show the ground state of CrGeTe3 at the corresponding field. The external field cants the spins towards the plane. The dashed line represents the saturation field at which the spins lie completely in-plane [30, 41].

FIG. 3: (a) The magnetoelastic single magnon-phonon coupling rate gme and (b) zero point fluctuation xzpf vs. membrane length Lx and membrane width Ly for initial deformation h = 100 nm and thickness Lz = 20 nm. The contour lines are for visual guidance only.
FIG. 4: The magnetoelastic single magnon-phonon cou- pling rate gme vs. initial displacement and total defor- mation for Lx = Ly = 2 µm and Lz = 20 nm.

FIG. 5: (a) Mechanical deformation h and mechanical eigenfrequency as a function of applied gate voltage, ob- tained from COMSOL simulations. The inset shows eigenfrequency as a function of voltage from 0 to 4 V (b) Sin- gle magnon-phonon coupling strength as a function of the mechanical deformation h. The red line shows the ana- lytical result, which neglects the reduction of the mechanical eigenfrequency induced by electrostatic gating. The blue symbols correspond to COMSOL simulations.
